rationale: The page provides detailed legal and constitutional information regarding the appointment of Additional and District Judges, as well as the establishment, evolution, and jurisdiction of Tribunals in India. This content is highly suitable for generating multiple-choice questions on Indian Polity.
cleanedText:
Related News
Additional Judges
Recently, the President appointed 14 Advocates as Additional Judges of Bombay High Court.
About Additional Judges
- Appointed under: Article 224
- Time Period: Not exceeding two years and shall hold office till the age of 62 years.
- Reason: Any temporary increase in business of a High Court or by reason of arrears of work.
- Sanctioned Strength: Permanent and Additional Judges in the ratio of 75:25.
District Judges
Supreme Court held that judicial officers with combined experience of 7 years as an advocate and in service will be eligible for direct recruitment as District Judges.
- It held that Article 233 permitted both advocates and in-service candidates to be appointed as District Judges and extended the criteria of 7 years’ practice to judicial officers as well.
Appointment of District Judge
- Article 233 deals with appointment of District Judge.
- Appointment Authority: Appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Court
4.2. TRIBUNALS
Why in the news?
Recently, Supreme Court struck down Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021 relating to appointments, tenure, and service conditions of members of various Tribunals.
Tribunal System in India
- Genesis and Evolution: Recommended by 1st ARC in 1969. 42nd Amendment 1976 set up administrative tribunals based on Swaran Singh Committee. Finance Act 2017 re-organised Tribunals reducing their number from 26 to 19.
- Constitutional Basis: Article 323A (Administrative Tribunals; CAT established under this). Article 323B (Tribunals for other matters). Supreme Court, 2010: Matters under Article 323B are not exclusive; legislatures can create tribunals on subjects in the Seventh Schedule.
- CAT Jurisdiction: Service matters related to recruitment and conditions of service of public servants under the Union and its authorities.
- Jurisdiction: Defined subject-wise; some tribunals have appellate jurisdiction.
- Appeals: Lie with the High Courts; in some cases, directly to Supreme Court.
Differences between Tribunals and Courts
- Feature: Establishment
- Tribunals: By acts of Parliament. Inserted via 42nd CAA, 1976 by adding 323A (Administrative Tribunal both at centre and state level) and 323B (For other matters like taxation, land reforms, etc.). Recommended by Swaran singh committee.
- Courts: By Constitution of India only.
